subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Open your eyes in the morning and the first thing you meet is light. The strong equatorial sun climbs over the acacia trees, spills across the savanna, and paints long shadows on the ground. By the time you eat your breakfast of canjeero, those shadows have already begun to shrink. Light is everywhere in your day, and yet it is so ordinary that we hardly ever stop to ask what it really is. In this unit you are going to stop and look closely.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the Stage 3 science workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Name light sources and sort them into natural (the sun, stars, fire, lightning) and artificial (torches, lamps, phone screens, car headlights).
Explain that we need light to see, and describe what happens to your eyes in complete darkness.
Explain that light travels in straight lines and use this idea to say where a shadow will fall.
Describe how a shadow forms when an opaque object blocks light.
Sort materials into transparent, translucent, and opaque by testing them with a torch.
Change the size of a shadow on purpose by moving the object or the light, and explain why it changes.
Predict how shadows change through the day and connect this to the sun’s apparent movement across the sky.
Describe reflection — how light bounces off shiny surfaces such as a mirror or still water.
Here are the important words for this unit. Read them now, then come back whenever you meet one in the lesson and it feels fuzzy.

concepts.title: Open your eyes in the morning and the first thing you meet…
concepts.explanation: Open your eyes in the morning and the first thing you meet is light. The strong equatorial sun climbs over the acacia trees, spills across the savanna, and paints long shadows on the ground. By the time you eat your breakfast of canjeero, those shadows have already begun to shrink. Light is everywhere in your day, and yet it is so ordinary that we hardly ever stop to ask what it really is. In this unit you are going to stop and look closely.
concepts.example: You will discover that light travels in straight lines, that it comes from light sources, and that when something solid gets in its way a shadow is born. You will learn why you cannot see anything in a pitch-dark room,…

concepts.title: You will discover that light travels in straight lines,…
concepts.explanation: You will discover that light travels in straight lines, that it comes from light sources, and that when something solid gets in its way a shadow is born. You will learn why you cannot see anything in a pitch-dark room, why a goat lying under an acacia tree at noon has almost no shadow at all, and why the same goat has a shadow longer than a grown man in the late afternoon. You will sort everyday materials into three families — transparent, translucent, and opaque — by testing how much light each one lets through.
concepts.example: Light and shadow are woven through the whole of your life, morning and night. In the strong heat of the day, shade is not a small comfort but a real need. Camels, goats, and cattle crowd under the nearest acacia tree at…

concepts.title: Light and shadow are woven through the whole of your life,…
concepts.explanation: Light and shadow are woven through the whole of your life, morning and night. In the strong heat of the day, shade is not a small comfort but a real need. Camels, goats, and cattle crowd under the nearest acacia tree at noon, and people rest in the cool shadow of a wall or a doorway. At night, when the great natural light of the sun is gone, families reach for torches and lamps so they can eat, read, and move about safely. During Ramadan, the thin silver light of the new moon marks the beginning of the month. All…
concepts.example: This knowledge is not just for the classroom. Herders in Garissa read the length of shadows to guess the time. Families build shade shelters so their camels and goats can rest out of the burning midday heat. Fishermen…

concepts.title: This knowledge is not just for the classroom. Herders in…
concepts.explanation: This knowledge is not just for the classroom. Herders in Garissa read the length of shadows to guess the time. Families build shade shelters so their camels and goats can rest out of the burning midday heat. Fishermen on the Indian Ocean coast watch the sun sink and know when to head home. When you understand light and shadow, you understand a tool that people all around you use every single day. Looking carefully at how the world works is also a way of reflecting on the wonder of creation, and Muslims are…

concepts.title: 💡 Ask Your AI Tutor Tell your AI tutor: “Go outside with…
concepts.explanation: 💡 Ask Your AI Tutor Tell your AI tutor: “Go outside with me in your imagination. Describe what my shadow would look like right now if it is 8 o’clock in the morning.” Then ask: “Why will my shadow look different at noon?” Keep the conversation going until the idea feels clear.

explorations.title: How a Shadow Is Born
explorations.context: To find out where a shadow forms when an object blocks the light from a torch.
explorations.prompt: Before you begin, write down: On which side of the object do you think the shadow will appear — the same side as the torch, or the opposite side? Why do you think so?
explorations.answer: Did the shadow appear on the same side as the torch, or the opposite side? When you moved the torch, which way did the shadow move? Use the straight-line rule to explain why the shadow forms behind the object.
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out where a shadow forms when an object blocks the light from a torch.

explorations.title: Growing and Shrinking Shadows
explorations.context: To find out how the size of a shadow changes when you move the object between the torch and the wall.
explorations.prompt: Predict: When I move the object closer to the torch, will its shadow get bigger or smaller? Write your guess and your reason.
explorations.answer: When was the shadow biggest — near the torch or near the wall? Describe the pattern in your own words. Which one thing did you keep the same to make it a fair test?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out how the size of a shadow changes when you move the object between the torch and the wall.

explorations.title: The Three Material Families
explorations.context: To sort household materials into transparent, translucent, and opaque by testing how much light passes through each one.
explorations.prompt: Predict which of your materials will let light through clearly, which will let some light through, and which will block it completely.
explorations.answer: Which material let the most light through? Which let the least? Which materials made the darkest shadows, and why? Which material would be best for a window, and which would be best for a door? Explain.
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To sort household materials into transparent, translucent, and opaque by testing how much light passes through each one.

explorations.title: Make Your Own Sundial
explorations.context: To track how an outdoor shadow changes through the day and use it to tell the time, like a sundial.
explorations.prompt: Predict how your shadow-stick’s shadow will change from morning to noon to afternoon. Will it be longest in the morning or at midday?
explorations.answer: At what time was the shadow shortest? What does that tell you about where the sun was? In which direction did the shadow point in the morning, and in the afternoon? How could a person use your marked stones to guess the time tomorrow?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To track how an outdoor shadow changes through the day and use it to tell the time, like a sundial.

explorations.title: Bouncing Light with a Mirror
explorations.context: To explore how light reflects off a mirror and other surfaces.
explorations.prompt: Predict which surface will give the clearest reflection: a mirror, a metal spoon, a bowl of still water, or a piece of cloth.
explorations.answer: Which surfaces gave the clearest reflection? What did they have in common? Why does a rough cloth not show a reflection? How does this experiment help explain why the moon can light up the night?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To explore how light reflects off a mirror and other surfaces.

methods.title: How a Shadow Is Born
methods.example: To find out where a shadow forms when an object blocks the light from a torch.
steps: Darken the room by closing the curtains.
Stand the object about 20 cm in front of the wall.
Shine the torch at the object from the far side, so the object sits between the torch and the wall.
Look at the wall. Find the dark shadow and notice which side of the object it is on.
Move the torch to the left, then to the right, and watch how the shadow moves.

methods.title: Growing and Shrinking Shadows
methods.example: To find out how the size of a shadow changes when you move the object between the torch and the wall.
steps: Darken the room and shine the torch at the wall.
Hold the object halfway between the torch and the wall. Measure the height of its shadow with the ruler and record it.
Move the object close to the torch. Measure the shadow again.
Move the object close to the wall. Measure the shadow one more time.
Keep the torch still the whole time so the test is fair.

methods.title: The Three Material Families
methods.example: To sort household materials into transparent, translucent, and opaque by testing how much light passes through each one.
steps: Darken the room.
Hold each material in front of the torch, one at a time.
Look at how much light comes through, and whether you can see the torch clearly, dimly, or not at all.
Sort each material into transparent, translucent, or opaque.

methods.title: Make Your Own Sundial
methods.example: To track how an outdoor shadow changes through the day and use it to tell the time, like a sundial.
steps: Early in the morning, push the stick upright into the ground so it stands firmly.
Place a stone at the tip of its shadow. Measure the shadow’s length and write down the time.
Come back every two hours — mid-morning, noon, and afternoon — and mark the new shadow tip with a stone each time.
Measure the shadow at each visit and record the length, the time, and the direction it points.

methods.title: Bouncing Light with a Mirror
methods.example: To explore how light reflects off a mirror and other surfaces.
steps: In a darkened room, shine the torch at the mirror and watch where the light bounces to. Aim the reflected spot onto a wall.
Try to catch the reflected light and move it around the room by tilting the mirror.
Now look at your own reflection in the mirror, the spoon, the water, and the cloth.
Note which surfaces reflect a clear picture and which do not.

workedExamples.title: Reading a Shadow Table
workedExamples.prompt: Scientists record measurements in tables so they can spot patterns. Halima set a torch at one end of a table and shone it at a wall. She put a small wooden block in the beam and measured the height of its shadow as she moved the block to different distances…
workedExamples.solution: Block distance from the torch Height of the shadow

workedExamples.title: Guided example 2
workedExamples.prompt: We can see objects because light ______. (a) disappears (b) bounces off them into our eyes (c) turns into shadow (d) stays still
workedExamples.solution: 2. (b) light bounces off them into our eyes — we see reflected light.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 3
workedExamples.prompt: Light travels in ______. (a) curves (b) circles (c) straight lines (d) zigzags
workedExamples.solution: 3. (c) straight lines — light never bends on its own.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 4
workedExamples.prompt: A shadow forms when light is blocked by a(n) ______ object. (a) transparent (b) translucent (c) opaque (d) shiny
workedExamples.solution: 4. (c) opaque — only opaque objects block light fully to make a clear shadow.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: Which material is transparent? (a) cardboard (b) clear glass (c) aluminium foil (d) wood
workedExamples.solution: 5. (b) clear glass — you can see through it clearly.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: Which material is translucent? (a) a brick (b) a mirror (c) tissue paper (d) a metal sheet
workedExamples.solution: 6. (c) tissue paper — it lets some light through but scatters it.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: When is your shadow shortest? (a) early morning (b) around noon (c) late afternoon (d) at sunset
workedExamples.solution: 7. (b) around noon — the sun is highest, so shadows are shortest.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: To make a shadow bigger, move the object ______ the light. (a) closer to (b) further from (c) beside (d) away from
workedExamples.solution: 8. (a) closer to — closer to the light blocks more spreading rays, making a bigger shadow.

practice.answer: 1. (b) the sun — it is not made by people.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.answer: 2. (b) light bounces off them into our eyes — we see reflected light.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.answer: 3. (c) straight lines — light never bends on its own.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.answer: 4. (c) opaque — only opaque objects block light fully to make a clear shadow.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which material is transparent? (a) cardboard (b) clear glass (c) aluminium foil (d) wood
practice.answer: 5. (b) clear glass — you can see through it clearly.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which material is translucent? (a) a brick (b) a mirror (c) tissue paper (d) a metal sheet
practice.answer: 6. (c) tissue paper — it lets some light through but scatters it.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: When is your shadow shortest? (a) early morning (b) around noon (c) late afternoon (d) at sunset
practice.answer: 7. (b) around noon — the sun is highest, so shadows are shortest.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: To make a shadow bigger, move the object ______ the light. (a) closer to (b) further from (c) beside (d) away from
practice.answer: 8. (a) closer to — closer to the light blocks more spreading rays, making a bigger shadow.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Shadows change through the day because ______. (a) the shadow gets tired (b) the object moves (c) the sun appears to move as Earth spins (d) light changes colour
practice.answer: 9. (c) the sun appears to move as Earth spins.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which surface reflects light best? (a) a woolly blanket (b) a mud wall (c) a smooth mirror (d) rough cardboard
practice.answer: 10. (c) a smooth mirror — smooth shiny surfaces reflect best.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The moon is not a light source because it ______. (a) is too small (b) reflects the sun’s light instead of making its own (c) only comes out at night (d) is made of rock
practice.answer: 11. (b) it reflects the sun’s light instead of making its own.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: In the morning, when the sun is in the east, shadows point towards the ______. (a) east (b) west (c) up (d) north
practice.answer: 12. (b) west — shadows fall opposite the light source.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: How a Shadow Is Born
activities.materials: A torch (or a phone torch); A solid object such as a tin can, a mug, or a toy camel; A pale wall or a white sheet; A darkened room
steps: Darken the room by closing the curtains.
Stand the object about 20 cm in front of the wall.
Shine the torch at the object from the far side, so the object sits between the torch and the wall.
Look at the wall. Find the dark shadow and notice which side of the object it is on.
Move the torch to the left, then to the right, and watch how the shadow moves.

activities.title: Growing and Shrinking Shadows
activities.materials: A torch; A small object (a toy, a bottle cap, a spoon); A pale wall; A ruler or measuring tape
steps: Darken the room and shine the torch at the wall.
Hold the object halfway between the torch and the wall. Measure the height of its shadow with the ruler and record it.
Move the object close to the torch. Measure the shadow again.
Move the object close to the wall. Measure the shadow one more time.
Keep the torch still the whole time so the test is fair.

activities.title: The Three Material Families
activities.materials: A torch; A clear plastic bag or bottle; Tissue paper or a thin cloth; Cardboard; Aluminium foil; A drinking glass of water
steps: Darken the room.
Hold each material in front of the torch, one at a time.
Look at how much light comes through, and whether you can see the torch clearly, dimly, or not at all.
Sort each material into transparent, translucent, or opaque.

activities.title: Make Your Own Sundial
activities.materials: A straight stick, about as long as your arm; A sunny patch of open ground or sand; Small stones or chalk to mark the shadow; A measuring tape; A watch or clock to note the time
steps: Early in the morning, push the stick upright into the ground so it stands firmly.
Place a stone at the tip of its shadow. Measure the shadow’s length and write down the time.
Come back every two hours — mid-morning, noon, and afternoon — and mark the new shadow tip with a stone each time.
Measure the shadow at each visit and record the length, the time, and the direction it points.

activities.title: Bouncing Light with a Mirror
activities.materials: A small mirror; A metal spoon; A bowl of still water; A piece of cloth; A torch
steps: In a darkened room, shine the torch at the mirror and watch where the light bounces to. Aim the reflected spot onto a wall.
Try to catch the reflected light and move it around the room by tilting the mirror.
Now look at your own reflection in the mirror, the spoon, the water, and the cloth.
Note which surfaces reflect a clear picture and which do not.

terms: Light
Energy that lets us see. Travels in straight lines; can be reflected.
Light source
An object that makes its own light (sun, torch, candle, phone screen).
Natural light source
A light source not made by people (sun, stars, fire, lightning).
Artificial light source
A light source made by people (torch, lamp, headlight).
Shadow
A dark area made when an opaque object blocks light.
Opaque
Blocks light completely; you cannot see through it (wood, metal, cardboard).
Transparent
Lets light through clearly; you can see through it (clear glass, water).
Translucent
Lets some light through but scatters it (frosted glass, tissue paper).
Reflect
When light bounces off a surface (mirror, still water).
Sundial
A tool that tells time using the moving shadow of a stick.
commonMistakes: Thinking the moon makes its own light.
The moon has no light of its own.
Light travels only in straight lines.
Thinking a shadow is a dark substance.
Nothing dark comes out of the object.
Thinking you can see in total darkness.
Thinking the sun really moves across the sky.
The sun stays put; it only appears to move.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name two natural and two artificial light sources.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: ✅ Section B Answers 1. Natural: the sun, stars, fire, lightning (any two). Artificial: torch, lamp, car headlight, phone screen (any two). 2. There is no light in the room, so no light can bounce off objects into your eyes. Your eyes need light to see. 3. Light travels in straight lines from a…

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why can you not see anything in a completely dark room?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain in your own words how a shadow forms.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is the difference between a translucent and an opaque material?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Describe one way to make a shadow smaller.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.

reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.

questions.question: Which of these is a natural light source?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: the sun — it is not made by people.

questions.question: We can see objects because light ______.
options: bounces off them into our eyes
questions.answer: bounces off them into our eyes
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: light bounces off them into our eyes — we see reflected light.

questions.question: Light travels in ______.
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: straight lines — light never bends on its own.

questions.question: A shadow forms when light is blocked by a(n) ______ object.
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: opaque — only opaque objects block light fully to make a clear shadow.

questions.question: Which material is transparent?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: clear glass — you can see through it clearly.

questions.question: Which material is translucent?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: tissue paper — it lets some light through but scatters it.

questions.question: When is your shadow shortest?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: around noon — the sun is highest, so shadows are shortest.

questions.question: To make a shadow bigger, move the object ______ the light.
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: closer to — closer to the light blocks more spreading rays, making a bigger shadow.

questions.question: Shadows change through the day because ______.
options: the sun appears to move as Earth spins
questions.answer: the sun appears to move as Earth spins
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: the sun appears to move as Earth spins.

questions.question: Which surface reflects light best?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: a smooth mirror — smooth shiny surfaces reflect best.

questions.question: The moon is not a light source because it ______.
options: reflects the sun’s light instead of making its own
questions.answer: reflects the sun’s light instead of making its own
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: it reflects the sun’s light instead of making its own.

questions.question: In the morning, when the sun is in the east, shadows point towards the ______.
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: west — shadows fall opposite the light source.

selfAssessment: I can by the end of this unit, you will be able to:
I can name light sources and sort them into natural (the sun, stars, fire, lightning) and artificial (torches, lamps, phone screens, car headlights).
I can explain that we need light to see, and describe what happens to your eyes in complete darkness.
I can explain that light travels in straight lines and use this idea to say where a shadow will fall.
I can describe how a shadow forms when an opaque object blocks light.
I can sort materials into transparent, translucent, and opaque by testing them with a torch.
I can change the size of a shadow on purpose by moving the object or the light, and explain why it changes.
I can predict how shadows change through the day and connect this to the sun’s apparent movement across the sky.
